does: This paper introduces GTA-2, a hierarchical benchmark for evaluating general-purpose tool-using agents across two complementary dimensions. GTA-Atomic assesses short-horizon, closed-ended tool-use tasks requiring precise single-tool execution, while GTA-Workflow evaluates long-horizon, open-ended productivity tasks demanding complex multi-step workflows and real-world completion. The benchmark is grounded in authentic data: real user queries, deployed production tools, and multimodal contexts rather than synthetic or dummy tools. A novel recursive checkpoint-based evaluation mechanism decomposes open-ended objectives into verifiable sub-goals, enabling unified assessment of both model capabilities and the agent execution harnesses that orchestrate tool calls. Experiments reveal a pronounced capability cliff between atomic and workflow tasks, indicating substantial gaps in current agent architectures.
connects: This work extends prior research on tool-use benchmarking and agent evaluation frameworks that increasingly recognize limitations in synthetic evaluation settings. It builds on the original GTA benchmark while responding to documented misalignments between existing benchmarks and real-world requirements identified in recent tool-use studies. The paper directly addresses shortcomings of datasets relying on AI-generated queries and dummy tool environments, drawing on trends toward more authentic evaluation in language model assessment. It engages with broader literature on agent frameworks and execution harnesses for coordinating multiple tools, connecting to recent work on decomposing complex objectives and hierarchical task planning in multi-step reasoning.
enables: GTA-2 establishes infrastructure for developing and evaluating next-generation agents capable of real-world productivity workflows beyond toy task execution. The recursive checkpoint-based evaluation mechanism opens pathways for more fine-grained analysis of where agents fail in complex workflows and can guide targeted improvements in planning, tool selection, and error recovery. The capability cliff identified between atomic and workflow tasks points toward critical research directions in developing agents that gracefully handle multi-step coordination, context switching, and recovery from partial failures. The benchmark's grounding in deployed tools and authentic user queries enables industry-relevant agent development and facilitates knowledge transfer between academic models and production systems.
